<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <utility>

namespace test_reaction {

std::pair<bool, int> test_observer_notification(const std::string& observerName, const std::string& targetName) {
    // Create observer and target nodes
    auto observer = std::make_shared<TestObserver>(observerName);
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestObserver>(targetName);
    
    // Get the singleton observer graph instance
    auto& graph = ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Add observer relationship: observer watches target
    graph.addObserver(observer, target);
    
    // Trigger change on target, which should notify the observer
    target->valueChanged(true);
    
    // Return notification status and call count
    return {observer->wasChanged(), observer->getCallCount()};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

int test_reactive_calculation(int a, int b) {
    auto a_var = reaction::var(a);
    auto b_var = reaction::var(b);
    auto calculation = reaction::calc(a_var, b_var, [](int x, int y) { 
        return (x + y) * 2; 
    });
    return calculation.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

class TestObserverReset : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestObserverReset(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false), m_callCount(0) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount;
};

bool test_observer_reset(const std::string& observerName, const std::string& targetName) {
    auto observer = std::make_shared<TestObserverReset>(observerName);
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestObserverReset>(targetName);
    
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    graph.addObserver(target, observer);
    
    target->valueChanged(true);
    if (!observer->wasChanged() || observer->getCallCount() != 1) {
        return false;
    }
    
    int initialCallCount = observer->getCallCount();
    observer->reset();
    target->valueChanged(true);
    
    if (observer->getCallCount() != initialCallCount) {
        return false;
    }
    
    return true;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

class PersonRecord : public reaction::FieldBase {
public:
    PersonRecord(const std::string& name, int age) : m_name(name), m_age(age) {}
    
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }
    int getAge() const { return m_age; }
    
    void setName(const std::string& name) { m_name = name; }
    void setAge(int age) { m_age = age; }

private:
    reaction::Var<std::string> m_name;
    reaction::Var<int> m_age;
};

std::string test_reactive_fields(const std::string& initialName,
                                 int initialAge,
                                 const std::string& newName,
                                 int newAge) {
    PersonRecord person(initialName, initialAge);
    
    // Create a reactive calculation that depends on the person's fields
    auto info = reaction::calc([person]() {
        return person.getName() + " - " + std::to_string(person.getAge());
    }, person.m_name, person.m_age);
    
    // Update the fields
    person.setName(newName);
    person.setAge(newAge);
    
    return info.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

Please use functions from the `reaction` library to implement the `test_batch_execution` function.

The function should:
1. Create reactive variables
2. Create variable to track recomputation count
3. Create calculation
4. Perform non-batch updates
5. Record recompute count after non-batch updates
6. Create batch context
7. Execute batch
8. Record recompute count after batch updates
9. Return final sum and counts

Please output the complete contents of the file:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations

/**
 * Function: Test batch execution reduces recomputation count
 *
 * @param initialA Initial value for variable a
 * @param initialB Initial value for variable b
 * @param nonBatchA Value to set a without batching
 * @param nonBatchB Value to set b without batching
 * @param batchA Value to set a within batch
 * @param batchB Value to set b within batch
 * @return std::tuple<int, int, int> {final sum, count after non-batch updates, count after batch updates}
 */
std::tuple<int, int, int> test_batch_execution(int initialA,
                                               int initialB,
                                               int nonBatchA,
                                               int nonBatchB,
                                               int batchA,
                                               int batchB) {}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>

namespace test_reaction {

std::tuple<int, int, int> test_batch_execution(int initialA,
                                               int initialB,
                                               int nonBatchA,
                                               int nonBatchB,
                                               int batchA,
                                               int batchB) {
    auto a = reaction::var(initialA);
    auto b = reaction::var(initialB);
    auto recompute_count = reaction::var(0);

    auto sum_calc = reaction::calc([a, b, &recompute_count]() {
        recompute_count++;
        return a.get() + b.get();
    });

    // Non-batch updates
    a.set(nonBatchA);
    b.set(nonBatchB);
    int count_after_non_batch = recompute_count.get();

    // Batch updates
    {
        reaction::batch batch;
        a.set(batchA);
        b.set(batchB);
    }

    int count_after_batch = recompute_count.get();

    return std::make_tuple(sum_calc.get(), count_after_non_batch, count_after_batch);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

std::string test_named_reactions(int valueA, int valueB) {
    // Create named reactive variables
    reaction::Var<int> varA("varA", valueA);
    reaction::Var<int> varB("varB", valueB);
    
    // Create named calculation
    reaction::Calc<int> calcSum("calcSum", [&]() { 
        return varA.get() + varB.get(); 
    });
    
    // Create named action
    reaction::Action actionMul("actionMul", [&]() {
        int result = varA.get() * varB.get();
        // Action execution (result is unused)
    });
    
    // Retrieve names
    std::string nameA = varA.getName();
    std::string nameB = varB.getName();
    std::string nameCalc = calcSum.getName();
    std::string nameAction = actionMul.getName();
    
    // Combine names into a single string
    return nameA + "," + nameB + "," + nameCalc + "," + nameAction;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

instruction:
Please use functions from the `reaction` library to implement the `test_filter_trigger` function.

The function should:
1. Create reactive variable
2. Create variables to track recomputation count and last processed value
3. Create filter-triggered calculation
4. Apply filter
5. Update sensor with first value
6. Record recompute count after first update
7. Update sensor with second value
8. Record recompute count after second update
9. Get final status
10. Return final status and counts

Please output the complete contents of the file:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test filter trigger with conditional recomputation
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial sensor value
 * @param firstUpdate First update (expected to be within threshold)
 * @param secondUpdate Second update (expected to exceed threshold)
 * @param diffThreshold Minimum delta to trigger recomputation
 * @param levelThreshold Threshold to categorize HIGH vs NORMAL
 * @return std::tuple<std::string, int, int>
 *         {final status string, recompute count after first update, recompute count after second update}
 */
std::tuple<std::string, int, int> test_filter_trigger(double initialValue,
                                                      double firstUpdate,
                                                      double secondUpdate,
                                                      double diffThreshold,
                                                      double levelThreshold) {}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

Next, I have to track the recomputation count and the last processed value. So I'll need two variables: one for the count and another for the last value. Let's initialize them to 0 and some default, maybe NaN initially.

Then, create a filter-triggered calculation. The calculation should probably check the difference between the current and previous values. If the difference is above the threshold, then recompute. Also, there's a levelThreshold to categorize HIGH or NORMAL. So the calculation might look at the current value and compare it to levelThreshold to determine the status.

The reaction library has filters like ChangeFilter. So perhaps apply a filter to the sensor variable that checks if the absolute difference between the new and old values is greater than diffThreshold.

But how do I structure the calculation? The calculation function would take the current sensor value and the previous value (or the last processed value). Wait, but in reaction, calculations are typically based on reactive inputs. Maybe the calculation should depend on the sensor variable and use a filter to determine when to recompute.

Alternatively, the calculation could be a computed value that only updates when the sensor changes by more than the threshold. So using a filter on the sensor variable to trigger the computation.

Let me think: the sensor is a Var<double>. Then, create a Calc that depends on the sensor, but with a filter. The filter would check if the absolute difference between the current and previous sensor value is greater than diffThreshold. If so, then the calculation recomputes.

So the calculation function would take the current sensor value and compute the status. The status is "HIGH" if the value is above levelThreshold, else "NORMAL". But the calculation should only recompute when the sensor changes by more than diffThreshold.

Wait, but the problem says the filter is applied to the trigger. So the filter determines whether the calculation should recompute. So the filter function would return true if the change is significant enough (diff > diffThreshold), and then the calculation runs.

<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>
#include <cmath>

namespace test_reaction {

std::tuple<std::string, int, int> test_filter_trigger(double initialValue,
                                                      double firstUpdate,
                                                      double secondUpdate,
                                                      double diffThreshold,
                                                      double levelThreshold) {
    // Create reactive sensor variable
    auto sensor = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // Track recomputation metrics
    int recomputeCount = 0;
    double lastValue = initialValue;
    
    // Apply filter to sensor to only trigger on significant changes
    auto filteredSensor = sensor.filter([diffThreshold](double newValue, double currentValue) {
        return std::abs(newValue - currentValue) > diffThreshold;
    });
    
    // Create calculation that depends on filtered sensor
    auto statusCalc = reaction::calc([&](double currentVal) -> std::string {
        recomputeCount++;
        lastValue = currentVal;
        return currentVal > levelThreshold ? "HIGH" : "NORMAL";
    }, filteredSensor);
    
    // Update with first value and record recomputation count
    sensor.set(firstUpdate);
    int afterFirstUpdate = recomputeCount;
    
    // Update with second value and record recomputation count
    sensor.set(secondUpdate);
    int afterSecondUpdate = recomputeCount;
    
    // Get final status
    std::string finalStatus = statusCalc.get();
    
    return std::make_tuple(finalStatus, afterFirstUpdate, afterSecondUpdate);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

    // Create reactive calculation: var squared
    auto calc = reaction::calc([var]() { return var.get() * var.get(); });
    
    // Get initial values
    int initialExpressionValue = expr.get();
    int initialSquaredValue = calc.get();
    
    // Update the reactive variable
    var.set(baseValue + increment);
    
    // Get updated values
    int updatedExpressionValue = expr.get();
    int squaredValueAfterUpdate = calc.get();
    
    return CreateFactoryResult{
        initialExpressionValue,
        updatedExpressionValue,
        squaredValueAfterUpdate
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct BatchLifecycleResult {
    bool closedInitially;
    bool closedAfterClose;
    int recomputeCountAfterExecute;
};

BatchLifecycleResult test_batch_manual_lifecycle(int initialValue, int updatedValue) {
    auto var = reaction::var(initialValue);
    int recomputeCount = 0;
    auto calc = reaction::calc([&]() {
        recomputeCount++;
        return var.get() + 1;
    });
    
    reaction::Batch batch;
    
    // Add the variable update to the batch
    batch.set(var, updatedValue);
    
    bool closedInitially = batch.isClosed();
    
    batch.execute();
    
    int recomputeCountAfterExecute = recomputeCount;
    
    batch.close();
    
    bool closedAfterClose = batch.isClosed();
    
    // Ensure calculation is evaluated
    int calcValue = calc();
    
    return BatchLifecycleResult{
        closedInitially,
        closedAfterClose,
        recomputeCountAfterExecute
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

ThreadSafetyTransition test_thread_manager_manual() {
    auto& tm = reaction::get_thread_manager();
    tm.reset();
    tm.register_current_thread();
    std::size_t threadCount = tm.get_thread_count();
    
    bool beforeEnable = tm.is_thread_safety_enabled();
    tm.enable_thread_safety();
    bool afterEnable = tm.is_thread_safety_enabled();
    
    tm.reset();
    bool afterReset = tm.is_thread_safety_enabled();
    
    return ThreadSafetyTransition{
        beforeEnable, 
        afterEnable, 
        afterReset, 
        threadCount
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#include <reaction/core/resource.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ResourceAtomicResult {
    int finalValue;
    bool operationReportedChange;

<answer>```cpp
#include <cstddef>
#include <reaction/memory/stack_monitor.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct StackMonitorReport {
    bool monitoringEnabledBefore;
    bool guardAllocated;
    std::size_t usageDuringGuard;
    std::size_t usageAfterGuard;
    double usagePercentage;
    bool nearLimitDuringGuard;
    bool monitoringAfterDisable;
};

StackMonitorReport test_stack_monitor_guard() {
    StackMonitor::disable();
    StackMonitor::initialize();
    
    StackMonitorReport report;
    report.monitoringEnabledBefore = StackMonitor::isEnabled();
    
    {
        StackGuard guard;
        report.guardAllocated = StackMonitor::isGuardActive();
        report.usageDuringGuard = StackMonitor::getCurrentUsage();
        auto stats = StackMonitor::getStatistics();
        report.usagePercentage = stats.usagePercentage;
        report.nearLimitDuringGuard = stats.nearLimit;
    }
    
    report.usageAfterGuard = StackMonitor::getCurrentUsage();
    StackMonitor::disable();
    report.monitoringAfterDisable = StackMonitor::isEnabled();
    
    return report;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

SboStorageStatus test_sbo_storage_info(int initialSmall,

                                       int largeSeed) {
    // Step 1: Create lambda to generate large payload
    auto createLargePayload = [largeSeed]() -> LargePayload {
        LargePayload payload;
        for (auto& element : payload.data) {
            element = largeSeed;
        }
        return payload;
    };

    // Step 2: Create small SBOResource
    SBOResource<SmallPayload> smallResource{SmallPayload{initialSmall}};
    
    // Step 3: Create large SBOResource
    SBOResource<LargePayload> largeResource{createLargePayload()};
    
    // Step 4: Update small resource
    smallResource.update(SmallPayload{updatedSmall});
    
    // Step 5: Get storage information
    auto smallInfo = smallResource.getStorageInfo();
    auto largeInfo = largeResource.getStorageInfo();
    
    // Step 6: Return SBO storage status
    return SboStorageStatus{
        true,           // updateReportedChange (always true for successful update)
        smallInfo.isUsingSBO(),
        smallInfo.stackUsage(),
        largeInfo.initialized(),
        largeInfo.isUsingSBO()
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <reaction/concurrency/conditional_shared_mutex.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct SharedMutexResult {
    bool trySharedWithoutSafety;
    bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety;
    bool trySharedWithSafety;
    bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety;
};

SharedMutexResult test_conditional_shared_mutex() {
    auto &threadManager = ThreadManager::getInstance();
    threadManager.resetForTesting();

    ConditionalSharedMutex mutex;
    SharedMutexResult result;

    // Test without safety
    if (mutex.try_lock_shared()) {
        mutex.unlock_shared();
        result.trySharedWithoutSafety = true;
    } else {
        result.trySharedWithoutSafety = false;
    }

    // Test unique lock while shared is held (unsafe)
    {
        ConditionalSharedLockGuard guard(mutex);
        if (mutex.try_lock()) {
            mutex.unlock();
            result.tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety = true;
        } else {
            result.tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety = false;
        }
    }

    // Enable thread safety
    threadManager.enableThreadSafety();

    // Test with safety
    if (mutex.try_lock_shared()) {
        mutex.unlock_shared();
        result.trySharedWithSafety = true;
    } else {
        result.trySharedWithSafety = false;
    }

    // Test unique lock while shared is held (safe)
    {
        ConditionalSharedLockGuard guard(mutex);
        if (mutex.try_lock()) {
            mutex.unlock();
            result.tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety = true;
        } else {
            result.tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety = false;
        }
    }

    return result;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

}

}  // namespace test_reaction

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ResetBehaviorSnapshot {
    int beforeResetValue;
    int afterResetValue;
    bool safetyEnabledDuringReset;
};

ResetBehaviorSnapshot test_react_reset_behavior(int initialValue, int multiplier) {
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    auto& tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    tm.reset();
    
    // 3. Create a reactive variable
    auto sourceVar = reaction::Var<int>::create(initialValue);
    
    // 4. Create a calculation
    auto calc = reaction::Calc<int>::create([sourceVar, multiplier]() { 
        return sourceVar.get() * multiplier; 
    });
    
    // 5. Get value before reset
    int beforeResetValue = calc.get();
    
    // 6. Enable thread safety
    tm.enableThreadSafety(true);
    
    // 7. Check if thread safety is enabled
    bool safetyEnabled = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 8. Reset calculation logic
    calc.reset();
    
    // 9. Update source variable
    sourceVar.set(initialValue + 1);
    
    // 10. Get value after reset
    int afterResetValue = calc.get();
    
    // 11. Return reset behavior snapshot
    return ResetBehaviorSnapshot{
        beforeResetValue,
        afterResetValue,
        safetyEnabled
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#include <set>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace test_reaction {

struct OrderingSnapshot {
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    bool allUnique;
};

inline OrderingSnapshot test_react_ordering(const std::string &firstName,
                                            const std::string &secondName,
                                            const std::string &thirdName) {
    // Create reactive variables with the provided names
    auto var1 = reaction::React::var(firstName);
    auto var2 = reaction::React::var(secondName);
    auto var3 = reaction::React::var(thirdName);
    
    // Insert into a set to leverage React::operator< for ordering
    std::set<reaction::React> reactSet;
    reactSet.insert(var1);
    reactSet.insert(var2);
    reactSet.insert(var3);
    
    // Collect ordered names from the set
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    for (const auto& var : reactSet) {
        orderedNames.push_back(var.name());
    }
    
    // Check if all input names are unique
    bool allUnique = (firstName != secondName) && 
                    (secondName != thirdName) && 
                    (firstName != thirdName);
    
    return OrderingSnapshot{orderedNames, allUnique};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

class TrackingFieldNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit TrackingFieldNode(std::string name) : m_name(std::move(name)) {}
    
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
        reaction::ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }
    
    int notifications() const { return m_notifications; }
    
    void clear() { m_notifications = 0; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct FieldGraphCleanupResult {
    bool notifiedBeforeDelete;
    bool notifiedAfterDelete;
};

inline FieldGraphCleanupResult test_field_graph_cleanup(uint64_t objectId) {
    auto owner = std::make_unique<TrackingFieldNode>("owner");
    auto field = std::make_unique<TrackingFieldNode>("field");

    // Register nodes with the observer graph
    reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().registerNode(owner.get());
    reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().registerNode(field.get());

    // Get field graph and register field under the specified object ID
    auto& fieldGraph = reaction::FieldGraph::getInstance();
    fieldGraph.registerField(objectId, field.get());

    // Bind the field to the owner
    fieldGraph.bindField(field.get(), owner.get());

    // Trigger a notification on the field
    field->valueChanged(true);

    // Check if the owner was notified before cleanup
    bool notifiedBeforeDelete = (owner->notifications() == 1);

    // Clear the owner's notification count
    owner->clear();

    // Clear dependencies on the owner
    reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance().clearDependencies(owner.get());

    // Remove field mappings for the object ID
    fieldGraph.removeFieldMappings(objectId);

    // Attempt to re-bind the field (should fail due to cleanup)
    // fieldGraph.bindField(field.get(), owner.get()); // Optional: Uncomment to test re-binding

    // Trigger another notification on the field
    field->valueChanged(true);

    // Check if the owner was notified after cleanup
    bool notifiedAfterDelete = (owner->notifications() == 1);

    // Return the results
    return FieldGraphCleanupResult{notifiedBeforeDelete, notifiedAfterDelete};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

void CountingObserver::valueChanged(bool changed) {
    m_notifications++;
}

int CountingObserver::notifications() const {
    return m_notifications;
}

ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot test_observer_node_links() {
    auto observer = std::make_shared<CountingObserver>();
    auto source1 = std::make_shared<ObserverNode>();
    auto source2 = std::make_shared<ObserverNode>();
    auto source3 = std::make_shared<ObserverNode>();

    auto& graph = ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    graph.registerNode(observer);
    graph.registerNode(source1);
    graph.registerNode(source2);
    graph.registerNode(source3);

    source1->addObserver(observer.get());
    source1->notify(true);

    int firstNotificationsAfterInitial = observer->notifications();

    source1->removeObserver(observer.get());
    source2->addObserver(observer.get());
    source3->addObserver(observer.get());

    source1->notify(true);
    int firstNotificationsAfterUpdate = observer->notifications();

    source2->notify(true);
    int secondNotificationsAfterUpdate = observer->notifications();

    source3->notify(true);
    int thirdNotificationsAfterUpdate = observer->notifications();

    source1->close();
    source2->close();
    source3->close();
    observer->close();

    return ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot{
        firstNotificationsAfterInitial,
        firstNotificationsAfterUpdate,
        secondNotificationsAfterUpdate,
        thirdNotificationsAfterUpdate
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct LogicalExpressionSnapshot {
    bool initialAnd;
    bool initialOr;
    bool updatedAnd;
    bool updatedOr;
};

inline LogicalExpressionSnapshot test_logical_expression_builders(bool a, bool b, bool c) {
    // Create reactive boolean variables
    auto a_var = reaction::var(a);
    auto b_var = reaction::var(b);
    auto c_var = reaction::var(c);

    // Build logical expressions using operators
    auto and_expr = a_var && b_var;
    auto or_expr = a_var || b_var;

    // Get initial results
    bool initialAnd = and_expr.get();
    bool initialOr = or_expr.get();

    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    a_var = !a;
    b_var = !b;

    // Get updated results
    bool updatedAnd = and_expr.get();
    bool updatedOr = or_expr.get();

    return LogicalExpressionSnapshot{
        .initialAnd = initialAnd,
        .initialOr = initialOr,
        .updatedAnd = updatedAnd,
        .updatedOr = updatedOr
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <reaction/cache/cache_base.h>
#include <memory>
#include <chrono>

namespace test_reaction {

struct GenericCacheStatsSnapshot {
    std::size_t entriesBefore;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInsert;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate;
    std::size_t hitCount;
    std::size_t missCount;
    double hitRatio;
    bool firstLookupWasMiss;
    bool secondLookupWasHit;
};

class IntCache : public CacheBase<int, int> {
public:
    using CacheBase<int, int>::insert;
    using CacheBase<int, int>::lookup;
    using CacheBase<int, int>::getStats;
    using CacheBase<int, int>::invalidateAll;
    using CacheBase<int, int>::triggerCleanup;
};

GenericCacheStatsSnapshot test_generic_cache_base() {
    IntCache cache;
    
    // Get initial cache statistics
    auto initialStats = cache.getStats();
    const auto entriesBefore = initialStats.entries;
    
    // Perform initial lookup (expected to be a miss)
    const bool firstResult = cache.lookup(42);
    const bool firstLookupWasMiss = !firstResult;
    
    // Insert value into cache
    cache.insert(42, 100);
    
    // Perform second lookup (expected to be a hit)
    const bool secondResult = cache.lookup(42);
    const bool secondLookupWasHit = secondResult;
    
    // Get cache statistics after insertion
    auto postInsertStats = cache.getStats();
    const auto entriesAfterInsert = postInsertStats.entries;
    
    // Invalidate all cache entries and trigger cleanup
    cache.invalidateAll();
    cache.triggerCleanup();
    
    // Get cache statistics after invalidation
    auto postInvalidationStats = cache.getStats();
    const auto entriesAfterInvalidate = postInvalidationStats.entries;
    
    // Calculate hit ratio
    const double hitRatio = static_cast<double>(postInsertStats.hitCount) /
                           (postInsertStats.hitCount + postInsertStats.missCount);
    
    return GenericCacheStatsSnapshot{
        entriesBefore,
        entriesAfterInsert,
        entriesAfterInvalidate,
        postInsertStats.hitCount,
        postInsertStats.missCount,
        hitRatio,
        firstLookupWasMiss,
        secondLookupWasHit
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

instruction:
Please use functions from the `reaction` library to implement the `test_thread_manager_force_register` function.

The function should:
1. Get thread manager instance
2. Reset thread manager state
3. Check thread safety before registration
4. Register main thread
5. Check thread safety after main thread registration
6. Create worker thread and register it
7. Wait for worker thread to complete
8. Check thread safety after worker thread registration
9. Return force register snapshot

Please output the complete contents of the file:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *
 * Collects observable state around calls to ThreadManager registration APIs.
 */
struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

/**
 * Function: test_thread_manager_force_register
 *
 * Demonstrates how registering multiple distinct threads causes
 * ThreadManager to enable thread safety.
 *
 * - We start from a clean ThreadManager state.
 * - The main thread registers itself and remains the sole thread.
 * - A worker thread then also registers, which should trigger
 *   multi-thread detection and enable thread safety.
 *
 * @return ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *         A snapshot of the thread-safety flag before any registration,
 *         after the main-thread registration, and after the worker-thread registration.
 */
inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register();

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

messages.content:
Please use functions from the `reaction` library to implement the `test_thread_manager_force_register` function.

The function should:
1. Get thread manager instance
2. Reset thread manager state
3. Check thread safety before registration
4. Register main thread
5. Check thread safety after main thread registration
6. Create worker thread and register it
7. Wait for worker thread to complete
8. Check thread safety after worker thread registration
9. Return force register snapshot

Please output the complete contents of the file:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *
 * Collects observable state around calls to ThreadManager registration APIs.
 */
struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

/**
 * Function: test_thread_manager_force_register
 *
 * Demonstrates how registering multiple distinct threads causes
 * ThreadManager to enable thread safety.
 *
 * - We start from a clean ThreadManager state.
 * - The main thread registers itself and remains the sole thread.
 * - A worker thread then also registers, which should trigger
 *   multi-thread detection and enable thread safety.
 *
 * @return ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *         A snapshot of the thread-safety flag before any registration,
 *         after the main-thread registration, and after the worker-thread registration.
 */
inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register();

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

<think>Okay, I need to implement the `test_thread_manager_force_register` function using the `reaction` library's functions. Let me think about each step required.

First, the function needs to get the thread manager instance. From what I remember, the `ThreadManager` class probably has a static method like `getInstance()` that returns the singleton instance.

Next, reset the thread manager state. There should be a method like `reset()` or `clearState()` on the instance. Maybe `ThreadManager::getInstance().reset()`?

Then, check thread safety before any registration. The thread manager might have a `isThreadSafetyEnabled()` method. So we call that before any operations.

Register the main thread. The registration function might be `registerCurrentThread()` or something similar. So `ThreadManager::getInstance().registerCurrentThread()`.

Check thread safety again after main thread registration. Then create a worker thread. For that, I'll need to create a thread that also calls the registration function. But how to handle the thread creation? Maybe using std::thread, and in the thread function, call the registration.

Wait, but the worker thread needs to register itself. So inside the thread's lambda, we call `ThreadManager::getInstance().registerCurrentThread()`. Then, after starting the thread, wait for it to complete using `join()`.

After the worker thread finishes, check thread safety again. Finally, return a `ForceRegisterSnapshot` struct with the three boolean values.

Putting it all together:

1. Get the instance.
2. Reset state.
3. Capture safety before any registration.
4. Register main thread.
5. Capture safety after main registration.
6. Create worker thread that registers itself.
7. Wait for worker thread to finish.
8. Capture safety after worker registration.
9. Return the snapshot.

Potential issues: How does the worker thread know when to register? The lambda should just call the registration function immediately upon starting. Then, the main thread waits for it to complete.

Also, need to make sure that the thread manager's state is properly synchronized between threads. The `reaction` library's ThreadManager should handle that, but the test is designed to verify that.

So the code structure would look like:

```cpp
#include <thread>
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register() {
    auto& tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    tm.reset(); // assuming reset() exists
    
    bool safetyBefore = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    tm.registerCurrentThread();
    
    bool safetyAfterMain = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    std::thread worker([&tm]() {
        tm.registerCurrentThread();
    });
    worker.join();
    
    bool safetyAfterWorker = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    return ForceRegisterSnapshot{safetyBefore, safetyAfterMain, safetyAfterWorker};
}

} // namespace
```

Wait, but the function signature says to return `ForceRegisterSnapshot`, which has those three booleans. The names in the struct are `safetyBeforeForce`, `safetyAfterMainForce`, `safetyAfterWorkerForce`. So the variables in the code should match that.

Also, the function is inline, as per the original code. The worker thread's lambda captures the thread manager instance by reference. That should be okay since the thread manager is a singleton.

I need to confirm the actual method names from the `reaction` library. Since the user mentioned using functions from the `reaction` library, perhaps the thread manager has methods like `getInstance()`, `reset()`, `registerCurrentThread()`, and `isThreadSafetyEnabled()`.

Assuming those methods exist, the code above should work. The key points are:

- Resetting the thread manager to ensure a clean state.
- Checking thread safety before and after each registration.
- Creating a worker thread that registers itself.
- Waiting for the worker thread to complete before checking the final state.

This should demonstrate that when multiple threads register, the thread manager enables thread safety measures.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <thread>
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register() {
    auto& tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    tm.reset();
    
    bool safetyBeforeForce = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    tm.forceRegisterCurrentThread();
    
    bool safetyAfterMainForce = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    std::thread worker([&tm]() {
        tm.forceRegisterCurrentThread();
    });
    worker.join();
    
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    return ForceRegisterSnapshot{
        safetyBeforeForce,
        safetyAfterMainForce,
        safetyAfterWorkerForce
    };
}

} // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

instruction:
Please use functions from the `reaction` library to implement the `test_raw_expression_builders` function.

The function should:
1. Create reactive variables
2. Build sum expression
3. Build negated expression
4. Build chained expression
5. Get initial expression values
6. Update variables to trigger recomputation
7. Get updated expression values
8. Return raw expression snapshot

Please output the complete contents of the file:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: RawExpressionSnapshot
 *
 * Captures the values produced by raw expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) before and after updating
 * their reactive inputs.
 */
struct RawExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialSum;
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int initialChainedValue;

    int updatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    int updatedChainedValue;
};

/**
 * Function: test_raw_expression_builders
 *
 * Demonstrates how low-level expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) can be combined with
 * `expr` to build reactive calculations without using the higher
 * level `make_binary_op` / `make_unary_op` shortcuts.
 *
 * The function:
 * - Creates three reactive integers A, B, C.
 * - Builds sum = A + B via make_binary_expr.
 * - Builds negated = -(A + B) via make_unary_expr(make_binary_expr(...)).
 * - Builds chained = -(A + B) * C via nested make_* calls.
 * - Updates A and C and records all expression values before and after.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first input
 * @param b Initial value for the second input
 * @param c Initial value for the chaining multiplier
 * @return RawExpressionSnapshot
 *         Snapshot of sum / negated / chained values before and after updates.
 */
inline RawExpressionSnapshot test_raw_expression_builders(int a, int b, int c);

}  // namespace test_reaction
```
